content:
Why following strict writing patterns triggers false positives, penalizing ESL authors and those with GAD or OCD

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by RDNE Stock project at PexelsIt happened again:
The other day, after writing an article about the Constitution, I ran it through GPTZero because I’ve noticed that when you cite specific data or a famous quote that you searched on the internet, the program gives you a false positive AI score.
This is not my hunch or an excuse as a defensive writer; you will find the studies to prove my point further on.
As expected, it said it was 42% AI-written. So I decided to modify the paragraph through GPTZero, rewriting and editing it myself, inside the detector’s own workspace window. However, to my surprise, the score did not budge by a single percentage point; it went higher.
It was the same paragraph, polished through my own human filters and physically retyped. Yet, the machine called my human voice and text a lie. That was the moment when the idea for this article was born.
Have you ever felt frustrated after spending hours researching and writing an article or book from scratch, only for an AI algorithm to slap your work with an “AI-Generated” label?
